text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY AND BONE MARROW TRANSPLANT PROGRAM
OUTPATIENT CLINIC FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Post-Allogeneic Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplant — Day +45

---

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Hematology/Oncology, Bone Marrow Transplant Service
VISIT TYPE: Established patient, post-transplant follow-up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Margaret Chen is a 48-year-old female presenting for her first scheduled outpatient transplant clinic visit following discharge from the inpatient bone marrow transplant unit. She is now approximately 45 days post matched unrelated donor (MUD) allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (alloHSCT) for adverse-risk acute myeloid leukemia (AML). She was discharged home on day +28 and this is her first formal post-discharge assessment in our outpatient clinic.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Chen is a 48-year-old female biology teacher with a history of hypothyroidism who was diagnosed approximately three and a half months ago with de novo AML, NOS, with an adverse-risk molecular profile. At diagnosis, she presented with a white count of 87,400/uL with 40% circulating blasts, hemoglobin of 6.8 g/dL, and platelets of 32,000/uL, along with prominent gingival hyperplasia and bleeding. Bone marrow biopsy at diagnosis demonstrated 72% myeloblasts in a hypercellular marrow (95% cellularity). Immunophenotype was CD34+/CD117+/CD33+/CD13+/MPO+/HLA-DR+. Cytogenetics revealed a normal karyotype (46,XX). Comprehensive myeloid NGS panel showed a high allelic ratio FLT3-ITD (33 bp insertion, VAF 40.2%, allelic ratio 0.68) along with co-mutations in DNMT3A (p.R882H, VAF 48.1%), RUNX1 (p.S291fs*16, VAF 43.7%), and a subclonal TET2 nonsense mutation. The combination of high allelic ratio FLT3-ITD with NPM1-unmutated disease and RUNX1 frameshift placed this firmly in the ELN 2022 adverse-risk category.

She was treated with 7+3 induction chemotherapy (cytarabine 100 mg/m2/day continuous infusion days 1-7 plus daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 on days 1-3) with midostaurin 50 mg PO twice daily on days 8-21 per the RATIFY protocol. Her induction course was complicated by febrile neutropenia with bilateral lower lobe pneumonia requiring empiric cefepime and micafungin, and by grade 2 mucositis. Grade 2 nausea/vomiting attributed to midostaurin was managed with prochlorperazine. Day 28 bone marrow biopsy confirmed morphologic complete remission with less than 5% blasts (3%) and MRD negativity by 8-color flow cytometry. FLT3-ITD was undetectable by PCR.

Given her adverse-risk disease profile, she proceeded directly to allogeneic SCT without additional consolidation chemotherapy. Her one full sibling was a 7/10 HLA match and was not suitable as donor. Unrelated donor search identified a 10/10 HLA-matched (A, B, C, DRB1, DQB1) unrelated male donor, age 32, CMV seropositive — a well-matched donor. Pre-transplant evaluation confirmed ECOG PS 0-1, adequate cardiac function (LVEF 60%), normal pulmonary function (FEV1 88% predicted, DLCO 82%), and normal organ function.

She received myeloablative conditioning with fludarabine/busulfan (FluBu4), pharmacokinetically dosed targeting AUC 4000-5000 umol*min/L per day, with levetiracetam seizure prophylaxis during busulfan administration. Day 0 infusion of peripheral blood stem cells from the MUD occurred without incident; CD34+ cell dose was 5.2 x 10^6/kg. GVHD prophylaxis was tacrolimus (continuous IV infusion, trough target 5-15 ng/mL, transitioned to oral when tolerating PO) plus methotrexate 15 mg/m2 on day +1 and 10 mg/m2 on days +3, +6, and +11. CMV prophylaxis with letermovir 480 mg daily was started on day +1. Antifungal coverage with micafungin was continued throughout the aplastic phase. TMP-SMX was held during conditioning and the peri-engraftment period.

The post-transplant inpatient course was notable for grade 3 mucositis developing around day +8, which required TPN and IV morphine PCA for pain control, along with grade 2 nausea/vomiting and grade 1 diarrhea. Mucositis gradually improved and she was tolerating oral intake by day +18. A supratherapeutic tacrolimus level (25 ng/mL on day +10) required dose reduction and was associated with transient creatinine elevation to 1.4 mg/dL from a baseline of 0.8 mg/dL; renal function recovered to creatinine 1.0 mg/dL by day +20 with dose adjustment. Engraftment occurred with ANC recovery by day +18.

Day +30 bone marrow biopsy (see prior pathology report) confirmed engraftment: normocellular marrow (50-60% cellularity), less than 1% blasts, MRD negative by flow cytometry, and 46,XY karyotype in all 20 metaphases analyzed — confirming full donor chimerism. STR-based chimerism analysis showed 98% donor total chimerism (99% myeloid, 92% T cell). She was discharged on day +28 with a stable clinical status.

She presents today 17 days post-discharge for her day +45 follow-up. Overall, she reports feeling gradually better. Energy levels remain moderately reduced compared to her pre-illness baseline but are improved compared to discharge. She is able to perform all activities of daily living. She is spending most of her time at home. Her husband has been providing caregiver support.

She denies fever, chills, or rigors. She denies significant cough, shortness of breath, or chest pain. Appetite is improving; she is tolerating a soft to regular diet. She reports 2-3 loose stools per day, which she describes as her current baseline — no blood, no mucus, no significant cramping. She has not noticed any new skin rashes. She reports some residual oral dryness.

She is taking all her medications as prescribed. She notes occasional mild nausea, manageable without the need for additional antiemetics. She denies new rashes, visual changes, joint pain, or edema.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: Persistent mild fatigue, but improving week over week. Denies fever, chills, or night sweats.
Skin: No new rashes reported. No blistering, no pruritic papules. No pigment changes noted by patient.
Eyes: Denies visual changes, dry eye symptoms, or photophobia.
Oral/ENT: Mild xerostomia present since transplant. No active oral ulcers. No dysphagia.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity swelling.
Respiratory: No shortness of breath at rest or with minimal exertion. No cough. No wheezing.
Gastrointestinal: Loose stools 2-3 times per day, formed to semi-formed, no blood, no mucus. Mild intermittent bloating. Appetite returning to near-normal.
Genitourinary: No dysuria, hematuria, or frequency. Normal urine output.
Musculoskeletal: No joint pain or swelling. Mild diffuse muscle deconditioning noted subjectively.
Neurologic: No headache, numbness, tingling, or tremor. No change in cognition. Reports difficulty with sleep — insomnia (difficulty initiating sleep).
Hematologic/Lymphatic: No unusual bruising or bleeding. No lymphadenopathy noticed by patient.
Endocrine: Continues levothyroxine for hypothyroidism, last TSH was normal.
Psychiatric: Mild anxiety about relapse and future health; coping adequately with support from husband and social work team.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. De novo AML, NOS — adverse risk (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, NPM1-unmutated, RUNX1 frameshift, DNMT3A R882H, TET2 subclonal) — currently in complete remission
2. Hypothyroidism — on levothyroxine, longstanding
3. No prior hematologic conditions, no prior MDS or myeloproliferative neoplasm
4. No prior solid tumors

PAST SURGICAL/PROCEDURAL HISTORY:
- Multiple bone marrow biopsies (diagnostic, day +28 assessment, day +30 post-transplant)
- Allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant (MUD, 10/10 matched, myeloablative FluBu4 conditioning, day 0 approximately 45 days ago)
- Tunneled central venous catheter (in place, used for all IV access throughout inpatient course)

SOCIAL HISTORY:
High school biology teacher. Married. Husband serves as primary caregiver. Lives in a suburban home with adequate caregiver support. Non-smoker with no history of tobacco use. Rare alcohol use, none since diagnosis. No illicit drug use. No significant occupational or environmental toxic exposures. Has not returned to work (on medical leave). Does not own pets (relevant given immunosuppression). Good sanitation practices in the home environment per transplant education reinforced at discharge.

FAMILY HISTORY:
No known family history of hematologic malignancy. Mother with breast cancer at age 65 (alive). Father deceased from coronary artery disease. One sibling (tested, 7/10 HLA match — not used as donor).

ALLERGIES:
No known drug allergies. No adverse reactions documented.

MEDICATIONS (current):
1. Tacrolimus (Prograf) — oral, trough-directed dosing, target 5-15 ng/mL (GVHD prophylaxis)
2. Letermovir (Prevymis) 480 mg PO daily (CMV prophylaxis)
3. Acyclovir 400 mg PO twice daily (HSV/VZV prophylaxis)
4. Fluconazole 400 mg PO daily (antifungal prophylaxis)
5. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (Bactrim DS) one tablet PO three times weekly (PJP/toxoplasma prophylaxis — restarted post-engraftment)
6. Levothyroxine — per pre-existing regimen
7. Ursodiol 300 mg PO twice daily (hepatic prophylaxis, veno-occlusive disease prevention, continuing per protocol through day +90)
8. Multivitamin without iron
9. Magnesium supplementation (oral) for tacrolimus-related hypomagnesemia

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 118/72 mmHg, heart rate 78 bpm and regular, respiratory rate 14 breaths per minute, temperature 37.0°C, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, weight 62.8 kg (baseline pre-treatment approximately 66 kg — still approximately 3.2 kg below pre-illness weight, but up 1.4 kg from discharge weight of 61.4 kg on day +28).

General: Well-appearing female in no acute distress. Alert and oriented times three. Appears mildly tired but overall looks well for the clinical context. No diaphoresis.

Head: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Thinning scalp hair diffusely (expected post-chemotherapy and conditioning). No seborrheic scalp changes.

Eyes: Conjunctivae pink, no icterus. Extraocular movements intact. No periorbital edema.

Oral/ENT: Mild xerostomia on examination. Oral mucosa intact with no ulcerations, no erythema, no lichenoid plaques, no pseudomembrane. Tongue normal. No gingival hyperplasia (completely resolved compared to at diagnosis). No adenopathy palpable in the submandibular or cervical chains.

Skin: Skin examination performed of face, trunk, bilateral upper and lower extremities. No rashes, erythematous plaques, papules, or blistering noted today. No macular discoloration. Mild dryness of skin diffusely, consistent with post-transplant changes and tacrolimus. Central venous catheter exit site on right chest wall: clean, no erythema, no discharge, no surrounding induration. Wound appears healthy.

Chest/Cardiovascular: Heart regular rate and rhythm, S1 and S2 without murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. No lower extremity edema.

Pulmonary: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No dullness to percussion. No crackles, wheezing, or rhonchi.

Abdomen: Soft, non-distended, non-tender to palpation. No voluntary guarding or rebound. Normoactive bowel sounds. Liver not palpably enlarged. Spleen: not palpable below the left costal margin — a substantial improvement from her presentation when the spleen was palpable 4 cm below the left costal margin.

Musculoskeletal: No joint effusions or swelling. Mild generalized muscle deconditioning expected in the post-transplant period. No bony tenderness.

Neurologic: Alert and oriented. No cranial nerve deficits. No tremor at rest or with intention (tacrolimus-related tremor not evident). No gait abnormality assessed. Sensation intact to gross testing.

Lymphadenopathy: No cervical, axillary, or inguinal lymphadenopathy appreciated.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained today, day +45):

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 4.8 k/uL
- ANC: 2,800/uL
- Hemoglobin: 10.9 g/dL
- MCV: 91 fL
- Platelets: 112,000/uL
Comment: CBC shows continued engraftment with ANC and platelet counts within acceptable post-transplant range. Mild anemia likely reflects ongoing recovery and erythroid maturation; no transfusion required.

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L
- Potassium: 3.6 mEq/L
- Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L
- Creatinine: 1.0 mg/dL (at pre-transplant baseline)
- BUN: 16 mg/dL
- Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL
- Direct bilirubin: 0.2 mg/dL
- ALT: 31 U/L
- AST: 27 U/L
- Alkaline phosphatase: 68 U/L
- Albumin: 3.5 g/dL
Comment: Liver function tests are within normal limits. Bilirubin, transaminases, and alkaline phosphatase all reassuringly normal, reducing concern for hepatic GVHD or sinusoidal obstruction syndrome at this time point. Renal function back to baseline.

Tacrolimus Trough Level (drawn this morning, pre-dose):
- 9.2 ng/mL (therapeutic range 5-15 ng/mL)
Comment: Tacrolimus is within the target therapeutic window. No dose adjustment needed.

Magnesium: 1.9 mg/dL (low-normal; oral supplementation ongoing)

LDH: 162 U/L (normal)

CMV PCR (peripheral blood): Undetectable (<200 copies/mL) — on letermovir prophylaxis

EBV PCR (peripheral blood): Undetectable

Donor Chimerism (peripheral blood STR analysis, drawn day +44):
- Total chimerism: 98% donor
- CD3+ T cell chimerism: 92% donor
- CD33+ myeloid chimerism: 99% donor
Comment: Full donor chimerism maintained with high myeloid engraftment. T cell chimerism at 92% is acceptable at day +45 for this GVHD prophylaxis regimen with tacrolimus and MTX; gradual increase in T cell chimerism is expected over time.

MRD Assessment: Not re-assessed peripherally today; will be assessed at next scheduled bone marrow biopsy or per clinical indication.

---

IMAGING AND OTHER STUDIES:
No imaging performed at this visit. Most recent imaging: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast performed at day +90 is pending (scheduled); the last available imaging is from the pre-transplant PET-CT which showed complete metabolic response with no FDG-avid lesions and normalized spleen size. Day +30 bone marrow biopsy (see prior pathology report): normocellular marrow, complete donor chimerism by STR, MRD negative.

---

GVHD ASSESSMENT:
Skin: Careful full skin exam performed. No erythematous papules, plaques, or maculopapular eruption. No lichen planus-like or lichenoid changes identified today. No desquamation, blistering, or tightening of skin. Assessment: no active cutaneous GVHD at this visit.

Oral Mucosa: Mild xerostomia present. No ulcerations, no lichen planus-like mucosal changes, no pseudomembrane. Assessment: no clinical oral GVHD; xerostomia may reflect early mucosal changes vs. post-conditioning effects.

Gastrointestinal: Loose stools 2-3 times daily, soft to semi-formed, no blood or mucus. No nausea, vomiting, or abdominal cramping at this time. Assessment: the diarrhea is at the lower end of what may represent GI GVHD vs. post-conditioning dysmotility; we are monitoring closely. No intervention required at this time.

Hepatic: Liver function tests normal. Bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL. Assessment: no hepatic GVHD.

Lungs: Clear to auscultation, no dyspnea. Assessment: no pulmonary GVHD.

Overall NIH GVHD assessment: No clinically overt acute or chronic GVHD at this time point (day +45). Patient will continue to be monitored vigilantly.

---

INFECTIOUS STATUS:
- Afebrile at this visit and at home per patient report
- No respiratory symptoms suggesting viral URI or pneumonia
- CMV PCR: undetectable on letermovir — excellent viral suppression
- EBV PCR: undetectable — no concern for post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder (PTLD) at this time
- Acyclovir prophylaxis ongoing for HSV/VZV coverage
- Fluconazole prophylaxis ongoing for fungal coverage
- TMP-SMX ongoing for PJP prophylaxis
- Central line site: clean, no signs of infection

---

CANCER STATUS:
Margaret is currently in complete remission from her adverse-risk AML (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, DNMT3A/RUNX1/TET2 co-mutations) following matched unrelated donor allogeneic stem cell transplant with myeloablative FluBu4 conditioning. Day +30 bone marrow biopsy confirmed morphologic and MRD-negative complete remission with full donor chimerism (46,XY karyotype confirmed). Peripheral blood chimerism today continues to show 98% donor chimerism with 99% myeloid engraftment. CBC is consistent with stable engraftment. No clinical features of relapse.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. AML IN COMPLETE REMISSION, STATUS POST MUD ALLOhsct (DAY +45):
Ms. Chen has had an excellent early post-transplant course. She achieved full engraftment by day +18, was discharged on day +28, and now presents for her day +45 follow-up with intact CBC, full donor chimerism, and no evidence of disease. Her adverse-risk molecular disease (high FLT3-ITD allelic ratio, NPM1-unmutated, RUNX1 frameshift, DNMT3A co-mutation) made allogeneic transplant in first CR the standard recommendation, and she has navigated induction and transplant successfully.
- Continue routine post-transplant surveillance
- Routine post-transplant CBC and metabolic labs will continue at each visit (twice-weekly for now, then transitioning to weekly as counts stabilize further)
- CMV and EBV PCR monitoring will continue weekly per protocol
- Next bone marrow biopsy planned at day +90 (approximately 6 weeks from today) for formal response and chimerism re-assessment; will include MRD by flow cytometry and molecular PCR for FLT3-ITD
- CT chest/abdomen/pelvis scheduled at approximately day +90 per our standard surveillance protocol

2. GVHD PROPHYLAXIS — TACROLIMUS:
Tacrolimus trough level therapeutic at 9.2 ng/mL. Continue current dose with plan to re-check level next visit. No dose adjustment today. Tacrolimus taper will not begin before day +100 per protocol, and only in the absence of active GVHD. Monitor closely for emerging GVHD signs at every visit — patient educated on self-monitoring for skin rash, new oral lesions, increasing diarrhea, and jaundice.
- Continue tacrolimus at current dose
- Taper to begin no earlier than day +100, contingent on continued absence of GVHD
- Re-check tacrolimus trough at next visit

3. CMV PROPHYLAXIS — LETERMOVIR:
CMV PCR undetectable on letermovir (Prevymis) 480 mg daily. Continue letermovir through day +100 per standard protocol. CMV PCR to be checked weekly.

4. INFECTIOUS DISEASE PROPHYLAXIS:
Continue all current prophylactic regimens:
- Acyclovir (HSV/VZV)
- Fluconazole (antifungal)
- TMP-SMX (PJP/toxoplasma)
- Ursodiol through day +90
No vaccinations at this time; live vaccines are formally contraindicated, and inactivated vaccines will be re-initiated per transplant vaccination schedule beginning approximately 6 months post-transplant.

5. GASTROINTESTINAL SYMPTOMS / LOOSE STOOLS:
Patient has loose stools 2-3 times per day without blood or mucus. This is being attributed to post-conditioning GI dysmotility and possibly a very early GI response to the transplant milieu. Liver function tests are normal. We will monitor the frequency and character of stools closely over the coming weeks. If there is an increase in stool volume, onset of bloody or watery diarrhea, or development of cramping, we would escalate evaluation for GI GVHD (including flexible sigmoidoscopy with biopsy). For now, no empiric immunosuppression augmentation is indicated.
- Dietary modifications: continue low-microbial diet, ensure adequate hydration
- Probiotic use: deferred per transplant protocol (immunocompromised, bacteremia risk)
- Monitor at next visit

6. ANEMIA / CBC RECOVERY:
Hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL, improving steadily from discharge. No transfusion required. Mild anemia is expected in the early post-transplant period due to ongoing erythroid reconstitution from donor hematopoiesis. Iron studies and B12/folate may be checked if anemia persists or worsens beyond expected trajectory. Patient tolerating activity with mild fatigue only.

7. TACROLIMUS-RELATED HYPOMAGNESEMIA:
Magnesium 1.9 mg/dL, low-normal. Continue oral magnesium supplementation. Recheck at next laboratory draw. Renal function normal.

8. INSOMNIA:
Patient reports difficulty initiating sleep. This is a very common complaint post-transplant, multifactorial in etiology (anxiety, disrupted sleep architecture from hospitalization, corticosteroid exposure, medications). Will refer to transplant psychology/behavioral medicine for sleep hygiene counseling. Melatonin 5 mg at bedtime may be initiated as a first-line option. Will avoid hypnotics in this early post-transplant period.

9. SURVIVORSHIP AND PSYCHOSOCIAL:
Ms. Chen is coping well overall with good support from her husband. Mild relapse-related anxiety is expected and was acknowledged. She has been connected with our transplant social worker and peer support program. She is on appropriate medical leave from her teaching position. Physical activity: gentle walking encouraged — target 20-30 minutes per day as tolerated to rebuild strength and endurance. Formal physical therapy or occupational therapy referral can be considered if deconditioning does not improve spontaneously.

10. CENTRAL VENOUS CATHETER:
Central line is in place and functioning well. Plan to discuss line removal once patient is fully stable on oral medications and no longer requires frequent IV access — likely within the next 2-4 weeks if CBC continues to recover and there are no complications requiring IV therapy.

RETURN TO CLINIC:
Patient to return in one week for repeat CBC, metabolic panel, tacrolimus trough, CMV/EBV PCR, and clinical reassessment. Thereafter, visit frequency will be adjusted based on clinical stability. Patient instructed to present to the emergency department or call the transplant on-call service for fever of 38.0°C or above, shaking chills, new rash, new oral lesions, significant increase in diarrhea (>5 stools per day or bloody stools), new shortness of breath, significant pain, or any other alarming symptom. She verbalized understanding of all return precautions. Contact information reviewed.

Physician signature: Hematology/Oncology, Bone Marrow Transplant Program
Visit duration: 60 minutes (clinical assessment, counseling, and care coordination)
